props.command: quiz me on chapter 5 — and don't go easy
output: spaced schedule — one week, one month out

props.heading: Why quizzing works.
props.body: When you read a chapter, the information goes in. When you're asked to retrieve it — without looking — you force the memory to reconstruct the information. That reconstruction is what makes the memory durable. Retrieval practice is one of the most replicated findings in cognitive psychology.

props.heading: Try it now — this is the technique itself.
props.body: Before the explanation arrives, state: what is the name of the cognitive science finding that makes retrieval practice effective? Commit to an answer in your head. The act of attempting the answer, even if you're wrong, is the learning event.

props.heading: Space the repetitions.
props.body: Quiz on new material today. Return to it tomorrow. Again in a week. Again in a month. The spacing curve is well-documented: each repeated retrieval at the right interval extends how long you'll be able to recall the material. Claude can run this schedule if you tell it when to ask.

props.heading: The explain-it-back move.
props.body: You tell Claude the concept — not Claude telling you. When you try to explain something and hit a gap, that gap is exactly what you need to study. Claude then fills the specific gap you identified, not a generic overview. This is more efficient than re-reading.

props.heading: Same moves, different career stage.
props.body: If you're a working professional dealing with new regulation, a new platform, or a new methodology — not a student — the quizzing loop is identical. Tell Claude the topic you need to learn. Have it quiz you before you've fully read the material. The gaps it reveals are your study list.

artifactLines: Quiz not answer: you do the retrieval, Claude is the question generator. Testing beats rereading — the testing effect is real.
Commit before the reveal: predict first, then see the answer. Commitment drives encoding. After-the-fact recognition is not the same.
Space the reviews: today · +1 day · +1 week · +1 month. Memory decays — spacing catches it at the fade.
Explain it back: tell Claude what you know; it shows you the gaps. Hesitation is information.

props.command: Quiz me on [topic or chapter]. Ask one question at a time. Wait for my answer. Tell me if I'm right and why. After 5 questions, give me a spaced review schedule: when should I quiz again to lock this in?
